trips.name: Amalfi Coast Drive
trips.description: One of the world's most celebrated coastal drives winds along SS163 past pastel-coloured villages clinging to limestone cliffs above a brilliant blue sea. From Salerno to Positano, every bend reveals cascading lemon groves, hidden coves, and vertiginous terraces draped in bougainvillea. This is la dolce vita distilled into a single unforgettable road.
trips.cover_search_query: Positano cliffside village Amalfi Coast Italy

trips.name: Naples, Pompeii & Vesuvius
trips.description: Naples sprawls beneath the brooding volcano that buried its ancient rival in 79 AD — and this loop brings you face to face with both. Walk the eerie streets of Pompeii, hike the crater rim of Vesuvius for panoramic views over the Bay of Naples, then return to the city for pizza margherita at its birthplace. History, drama, and the finest street food in Italy.
trips.cover_search_query: Pompeii ruins with Mount Vesuvius Italy

trips.name: Cilento Wild Coast
trips.description: South of the Amalfi crowds lies the Cilento, a UNESCO-protected wilderness of sea caves, waterfalls, and ancient Greek temples that most travellers never find. The coastal road weaves between fishing villages with turquoise grottos and mountainous interior parks where wolves still roam. Finish at the spectacular Greek temples of Paestum standing golden in the afternoon light.
trips.cover_search_query: Paestum Greek temples Cilento Campania Italy

trips.name: Irpinia Wine Hills
trips.description: Inland Campania's Irpinia hills produce some of southern Italy's most serious wines — Taurasi, Fiano di Avellino, and Greco di Tufo — from volcanic soils swept by Apennine breezes. Medieval villages perch on ridges above chestnut forests, and family-run cellars welcome visitors with the informality that only Italy's lesser-known wine roads provide. A superb alternative to more crowded Tuscan wine routes.
trips.cover_search_query: Irpinia vineyards hills Campania Italy

trips.name: Classic Siena Circuit
trips.description: This loop around one of Italy's most perfectly preserved medieval cities takes in Siena's shell-shaped Piazza del Campo, the cathedral striped in marble, and the rolling wheat fields that Ambrogio Lorenzetti painted in the 1300s — unchanged to this day. San Gimignano's towers appear on the horizon like a medieval Manhattan, and Monteriggioni's circular walls stand as intact as the day they were built.
trips.cover_search_query: Siena Piazza del Campo medieval Tuscany Italy

trips.name: Val d'Orcia & Crete Senesi
trips.description: The Val d'Orcia is the landscape painting come to life — rolling clay hills dotted with isolated farmhouses, lone cypresses, and hilltop towns that have barely changed since the Renaissance. The Crete Senesi to the north are equally haunting, vast badland ridges of pale clay rippling toward the horizon in the golden afternoon light. This is the Tuscany of postcards, and it absolutely lives up to the promise.
trips.cover_search_query: Val d'Orcia cypress trees rolling hills Tuscany Italy

trips.name: Chianti Wine Road
trips.description: The Chiantigiana road (SR222) threads through the heartland of Tuscany's most famous wine region, passing stone farmhouses converted into prestigious estates, olive groves silvering in the breeze, and medieval villages where Sangiovese grapes have been pressed for centuries. Castle after castle rises from the vine-covered hillsides — Brolio, Verrazzano, Vignamaggio — each with cellars open for tasting.
trips.cover_search_query: Chianti vineyards hilltop village Tuscany Italy

trips.name: Florence & Renaissance Villages
trips.description: Florence is the cradle of the Renaissance, and its surrounding hills are dotted with the villas, gardens, and hilltop towns that inspired Botticelli, Leonardo, and Michelangelo. Fiesole crowns a hill above the city with Etruscan walls and a Roman theatre; Lucca's Renaissance walls are intact and wide enough to cycle; and Pistoia punches far above its weight with Della Robbia ceramics at every corner.
trips.cover_search_query: Florence Cathedral Duomo Tuscany Italy

trips.name: Maremma & Etruscan Tuscany
trips.description: The Maremma is Tuscany's wild side — a coastal lowland of wetlands, butteri cowboys, medieval hill towns, and some of Italy's best undiscovered beaches. The Etruscan civilization flourished here 2,500 years ago, and their extraordinary necropolises at Pitigliano, Sovana, and Sorano — cities carved directly from volcanic tufa — rank among Italy's most atmospheric ancient sites.
trips.cover_search_query: Pitigliano tufa city Maremma Tuscany Italy

trips.name: Tuscan Riviera & Elba
trips.description: The Tuscan coast stretches from the fashionable Versilia beaches at Forte dei Marmi to the wild promontory of Monte Argentario, with a ferry crossing to Napoleon's island of exile — Elba — as the centrepiece. Elba's dramatic scenery, crystal-clear water, and excellent seafood make it one of Italy's finest island detours, while the mainland offers marble quarry towns, medieval towers, and Livorno's famous fish market.
trips.cover_search_query: Portoferraio harbour Elba Island Tuscany Italy

trips.name: Southern Tuscany & Terme
trips.description: The southern tip of Tuscany offers a more relaxed pace — natural hot springs bubbling freely from the earth at Saturnia, the perfectly preserved medieval hill town of Capalbio, and the wildlife-rich Monte Argentario peninsula jutting into the Tyrrhenian Sea. Tiny Sant'Antimo abbey sits in a valley of olive trees, and Scansano's Morellino di Scansano wine is one of Italy's best kept secrets.
trips.cover_search_query: Saturnia thermal waterfall Tuscany Italy

trips.name: Arezzo, Cortona & the Casentino
trips.description: Eastern Tuscany centres on Arezzo, whose Piazza Grande hosts one of Italy's great antiques fairs and whose churches shelter Piero della Francesca's cycle of frescoes on the Legend of the True Cross. Above the Arno's upper valley, Cortona perches on a steep hillside with Etruscan tombs in the surrounding terraced countryside. The Casentino forest — one of Italy's most ancient — hides Camaldoli hermitage and La Verna, where St Francis received the stigmata.
trips.cover_search_query: Cortona hilltop town Tuscany Italy

trips.name: Baroque Southeast Sicily
trips.description: After the 1693 earthquake flattened eight cities simultaneously, they were rebuilt in an extravagant Sicilian Baroque style unlike anywhere else in Italy — UNESCO has recognised the whole Val di Noto as a World Heritage Site. Syracuse was one of the ancient world's greatest cities, with Greek theatre, Roman amphitheatre, and the mysterious Ear of Dionysius cave all clustered on the island of Ortigia.
trips.cover_search_query: Noto Baroque cathedral Sicily Italy

trips.name: Western Sicily & Valley of the Temples
trips.description: The western tip of Sicily was Phoenician and Carthaginian before becoming Roman — a heritage visible in the astonishing ancient salt pans, tuna fishing traditions, and the greatest collection of Greek temples outside Greece itself. Agrigento's Valley of the Temples glows gold at sunset when the Temple of Concordia stands silhouetted against the sky in one of antiquity's most stirring images.
trips.cover_search_query: Valley of the Temples Agrigento Sicily Italy

trips.name: Taormina & Mount Etna
trips.description: Taormina's ancient Greek theatre commands the most theatrical backdrop imaginable — snowcapped Etna steaming on the horizon above the Ionian Sea far below. The volcano itself is the showstopper: cable car rides to the crater rim, lava-field hikes, and wine estates producing remarkable Nerello Mascalese reds from volcanic soils on Etna's slopes. The Alcantara Gorge cuts through black basalt lava to create a natural cathedral of rock.
trips.cover_search_query: Taormina Greek theatre Mount Etna Sicily Italy

trips.name: Palermo & the Conca d'Oro
trips.description: Palermo is one of Italy's most layered and chaotic cities — Arab-Norman churches sit beside Baroque piazzas and North African street markets that have operated since the Emirate of Sicily in the 9th century. The golden shell of the Conca d'Oro valley behind the city holds the breathtaking cathedral of Monreale with its 6,000 square metres of Byzantine mosaic gold. Cefalù's Norman cathedral rising from a dramatic rocky headland completes the picture.
trips.cover_search_query: Palermo Cathedral Arab-Norman Sicily Italy

trips.name: Tyrrhenian North Coast
trips.description: Sicily's north coast between Palermo and Messina is a sun-baked succession of Greek ruins, Norman castles, and fishing villages where the tuna mattanza has been practised for a thousand years. The Aeolian Islands shimmer on the horizon from every headland, and Milazzo serves as the ferry port. Tyndaris's cliff-top Greek theatre and the Laghetti di Marinello lagoon are among Italy's least-visited treasures.
trips.cover_search_query: Tindari Greek ruins north coast Sicily Italy

trips.name: Costa Smeralda & the Gallura
trips.description: The Costa Smeralda in northeast Sardinia is named for its emerald-green water — colour so improbable it looks digitally enhanced until you're standing ankle-deep in it. The Aga Khan developed the coast in the 1960s as a playground for the global elite, but beyond the superyacht harbours lie wilder beaches, pink granite boulders sculpted by the wind at the Maddalena Archipelago, and the fierce interior cork-oak forests of the Gallura.
trips.cover_search_query: Costa Smeralda emerald water Sardinia Italy

trips.name: Southern Sardinia
trips.description: Southern Sardinia concentrates the island's greatest contrasts in one arc — the elegant capital Cagliari rising above a pink flamingo lagoon, the spectacular white dunes of Piscinas, and the wild and windswept Peninsula of Sulcis. The sea here has a Caribbean quality that is hard to reconcile with being in southern Europe, and the island's archaeological heart — the nuraghi — are thickest in this region.
trips.cover_search_query: Villasimius beach crystal water Sardinia Italy

trips.name: Nuraghi Prehistoric Trail
trips.description: Sardinia's mysterious nuraghi — conical Bronze Age stone towers, of which 7,000 survive — give this island one of Europe's most compelling archaeological landscapes. The complex at Su Nuraxi di Barumini is the finest example, and the dramatic highland plateau of Gennargentu hides villages where traditional costumes are still worn at festivals. This is the authentic, time-forgotten Sardinia that existed long before the superyacht set arrived.
trips.cover_search_query: Su Nuraxi Barumini nuraghe Sardinia Italy

trips.name: Alghero & the Coral Riviera
trips.description: Alghero is Sardinia's most Catalan city — the language is still spoken in the old town's narrow alleyways, and the red coral for which the coast is named has been crafted into jewellery here since medieval times. To the north, the spectacular Capo Caccia cliffs plunge 165 metres into the sea above the extraordinary Grotta di Nettuno sea cave, accessible by a vertiginous staircase carved directly into the cliff face.
trips.cover_search_query: Alghero old town Sardinia Italy

trips.name: Salento: Italy's Deep South
trips.description: The Salento peninsula — the heel of Italy's boot — is fringed by two seas with such different characters that local people have always called them by colour: the turquoise Ionian on the west, the darker Adriatic on the east. Lecce is Puglia's Baroque showpiece, its honey-coloured sandstone so soft and workable that Baroque architects went berserk with decoration; the result is a city that makes Rome look restrained.
highlights: Santa Maria di Leuca land's end
trips.cover_search_query: Lecce Baroque cathedral Puglia Italy

trips.name: Trulli Country & Valle d'Itria
trips.description: The Valle d'Itria is the most singular landscape in Italy — a gentle valley whose every hill is crowned with trulli, the conical dry-stone houses that look like fairy-tale mushrooms sprouted from the limestone. Alberobello is the famous UNESCO site, but the whole valley from Martina Franca to Locorotondo is thick with these extraordinary structures, some centuries old, others converted into the most unique accommodation in Europe.
trips.cover_search_query: Alberobello trulli conical houses Puglia Italy

trips.name: Gargano Peninsula
trips.description: The Gargano is the spur of Italy's boot — a limestone massif jutting into the Adriatic, its ancient beech forest (the Foresta Umbra) rising to 1,000 metres above fishing villages with translucent water and white limestone caves. The pilgrimage sanctuary of Monte Sant'Angelo sits on the plateau above, and the Tremiti Islands are accessible by ferry for some of the Adriatic's clearest snorkelling.
trips.cover_search_query: Vieste sea stack Gargano Puglia Italy

trips.name: Bari & the Adriatic Coast
trips.description: Bari's old city is a labyrinthine tangle of whitewashed alleys where women still sit in doorways making orecchiette pasta by hand. The Basilica of San Nicola is one of southern Italy's finest Norman churches. The coast north and south of Bari is lined with Romanesque cathedrals in towns like Trani, Barletta, and Castel del Monte — a trail of ecclesiastical gems polished by medieval seafarers.
trips.cover_search_query: Polignano a Mare sea cliffs Puglia Italy

trips.name: Grande Strada delle Dolomiti
trips.description: The Grande Strada delle Dolomiti is one of Europe's legendary mountain drives — a route threading through the most dramatic limestone peaks on earth, past the Tre Cime di Lavaredo and the alpine meadows of the Alpe di Siusi. UNESCO recognised the Dolomites as a World Natural Heritage Site for their sheer geological improbability: vertical rose-coloured towers rising from green valley floors.
trips.cover_search_query: Dolomites mountains peaks lake reflection Italy

trips.name: Val Gardena & Sella Ronda
trips.description: Val Gardena is the Ladin-speaking heart of the Dolomites — a culturally distinct valley where Italian, German, and an ancient pre-Roman language are all spoken under the towering walls of the Sella massif. The Sella Ronda circuit circumnavigates the entire Sella Group across four mountain passes, making for a spectacular day's driving above the treeline through some of the most dramatic alpine scenery in Europe.
trips.cover_search_query: Val Gardena alpine village Dolomites Italy

trips.name: Tre Cime di Lavaredo Circuit
trips.description: The three prongs of the Tre Cime di Lavaredo are the Dolomites' most iconic image — and they are even more astonishing in person, rearing vertically from the surrounding plateau in a trio of sheer 300-metre faces. The classic circumnavigation hike takes four hours; the drive to the Auronzo refuge is one of the most dramatic toll roads in Europe. Lake Braies glows turquoise against the forest below.
highlights: Tre Cime di Lavaredo three peaks
trips.cover_search_query: Tre Cime di Lavaredo Dolomites Italy

trips.name: Alto Adige Wine Road
trips.description: The Alto Adige wine road follows the Adige valley south from Bolzano through a landscape that is equal parts Tyrolean Austria and Italian wine country — traditional Gasthäuser and apple orchards alongside Pinot Noir and Gewürztraminer vineyards. Caldaro lake is the warmest in the Alps and draws swimmers all summer; Merano has elegant Belle Époque thermal baths in a setting of pure Habsburg nostalgia.

stops.name: Appiano sulla Strada del Vino, Italy
stops.label: Appiano

stops.name: Caldaro sulla Strada del Vino, Italy
stops.label: Caldaro

trips.cover_search_query: Alto Adige wine road vineyards South Tyrol Italy

trips.name: Lake Como
trips.description: Lake Como's Y-shaped shoreline is lined with the grandest collection of Belle Époque and neoclassical villas in Italy, set against alpine peaks that plunge directly to the water's edge. Varenna and Bellagio divide the eastern and western arms at the lake's centre, and both villages are so extravagantly picturesque that it is difficult to take a bad photograph. George Clooney's villa at Laglio has only added to an already mythological reputation.
trips.cover_search_query: Bellagio Lake Como Italy

trips.name: Lake Garda Circuit
trips.description: Lake Garda is Italy's largest lake and most visited — and with good reason. Its southern shores bask in a Mediterranean microclimate that allows olive groves and citrus trees to flourish at the foot of the Alps; the northern end narrows between sheer limestone walls where the Peler wind blows with clockwork reliability every morning for windsurfers. Sirmione's narrow peninsula cuts into the southern end with Roman ruins and a fairy-tale Scaligeri castle at its tip.
trips.cover_search_query: Sirmione castle Lake Garda Italy

trips.name: Lake Maggiore & the Borromean Islands
trips.description: Lake Maggiore straddles the Italian-Swiss border beneath the Alps, its western shore thick with grand resort hotels and camellias that bloom in the mild air. The Borromean Islands are Maggiore's greatest spectacle: Isola Bella's palace rises in tiers of terraced gardens from the water like a stone ship; Isola dei Pescatori is a working fishing village of extraordinary beauty. The shores of the Verbano have been attracting European aristocracy since the 18th century.
trips.cover_search_query: Isola Bella Borromean Islands Lake Maggiore Italy

trips.name: Venice & the Lagoon
trips.description: Venice is unlike anything else on earth — a city of 118 islands connected by 400 bridges and served entirely by boat, where palaces in states of beautiful decay line a Grand Canal that has been the world's most glamorous waterway since the 14th century. The lagoon surrounding it contains more human history per square kilometre than almost anywhere: Byzantine mosaics at Torcello, colourful fishing houses at Burano, ancient glass-blowing at Murano.
trips.cover_search_query: Venice Grand Canal gondola Italy

trips.name: Veneto Wine Roads
trips.description: The Veneto produces more DOC wine than any other Italian region — from the prosecco hills of Conegliano-Valdobbiadene (now UNESCO listed) to Soave's volcanic basalt vineyards and the dark Amarone reds of Valpolicella. The wine roads wind through landscapes of vine-draped hillsides, medieval walled towns, and Palladian villas that formed the blueprint for country houses across the English-speaking world.
trips.cover_search_query: Conegliano Valdobbiadene prosecco hills Italy

trips.name: Verona, Vicenza & the Villas of Palladio
trips.description: Two of Italy's most beautiful smaller cities — Verona and Vicenza — anchor a drive through the Palladian landscape that changed the architecture of the western world. Vicenza is Palladio's home city and an open-air museum of his revolutionary buildings; the surrounding hills are dotted with the country villas he designed for Venetian patricians that directly inspired Thomas Jefferson's Monticello and every English country house thereafter.
trips.cover_search_query: Verona Roman Arena amphitheatre Italy

trips.name: Belluno & the Veneto Dolomites
trips.description: The province of Belluno is the gateway to the Dolomites from the Venetian plain — a landscape that transitions rapidly from the golden flatlands through the pre-Alps to the full Dolomitic glory of Cortina d'Ampezzo and the Cadore valleys. The Piave river cuts through gorges here, and the whole area carries the poignant memory of catastrophic First World War battles fought on these very ridges.
trips.cover_search_query: Cortina d'Ampezzo Dolomites alpine Italy

trips.name: Cinque Terre
trips.description: Five villages — Riomaggiore, Manarola, Corniglia, Vernazza, and Monterosso — are wedged into clefts and coves along one of Italy's most rugged coastlines, their colourful houses stacked up the cliffs above terraced vineyards. The famous Sentiero Azzurro coastal path connects them on foot; the train threads through tunnels in between. The Sciacchetrà sweet wine, made from grapes dried on these precipitous terraces, is a local treasure.
trips.cover_search_query: Manarola Cinque Terre colourful village Italy

trips.name: Portofino & the Riviera di Levante
trips.description: The Riviera di Levante east of Genoa is the more glamorous half of the Italian Riviera — Portofino's jewel-like harbour, Rapallo's belle-époque promenade, Santa Margherita's elegant seafront, and the headland of Punta Chiappa accessible only by boat or mule track. The Tigullio gulf shimmers turquoise, and everywhere the fishing boats are painted with the same bold colours that have been traditional here for centuries.
trips.cover_search_query: Portofino harbour Liguria Italy

trips.name: Riviera di Ponente
trips.description: The western Ligurian Riviera stretches from Genoa to the French border in a succession of flower-growing towns, sandy beaches, and cliff-top resorts that have attracted British and Russian aristocracy since the Victorian era. Sanremo hosts the Italian song contest every spring and has a casino and belle-époque opulence that feels transported from the 1920s; Albenga has a perfectly preserved Roman grid in its old town.
trips.cover_search_query: Sanremo Ligurian Riviera Italy

trips.name: Classic Umbria: Assisi, Perugia & Orvieto
trips.description: Umbria — Italy's green heart — lacks a coastline and has consequently been overlooked by mass tourism in ways that preserve its extraordinary medieval character. Assisi's Basilica of St Francis contains the greatest cycle of Giotto frescoes in existence; Perugia is a university city with renowned chocolate and Renaissance art galleries; Orvieto sits on a volcanic tufa cliff with a cathedral façade so encrusted with Gothic gold mosaic it seems to glow.
trips.cover_search_query: Assisi Basilica Saint Francis Umbria Italy

trips.name: Valnerina & Sibillini Mountains
trips.description: The Valnerina is the wild valley of the Nera river cutting through Umbria's most dramatic landscape — waterfalls, medieval hermitages, and saffron-growing villages clinging to cliff faces. The Monti Sibillini rise to over 2,400 metres above the Piano Grande plateau, a vast highland meadow that bursts into a carpet of wildflowers in June and draws pilgrims to the lentil fields of Castelluccio.
trips.cover_search_query: Piano Grande Castelluccio wildflowers Sibillini Italy

trips.name: Urbino & Renaissance Marche
trips.description: Urbino is the most perfectly preserved Renaissance city in Italy — a hilltop duchy where Federico da Montefeltro built a palace that Raphael was born in, and where the whole town remains essentially as it was in 1480. The surrounding Marche countryside is as beautiful as Tuscany but far less visited, with rolling hills, medieval towers, and farmhouse restaurants serving spectacular local cuisine built around black truffle, porchetta, and vincigrassi pasta.
trips.cover_search_query: Urbino Ducal Palace Renaissance Marche Italy

trips.name: Riviera del Conero & Sibillini Foothills
trips.description: Monte Conero rises as a white limestone headland directly from the Adriatic, its flanks covered in Mediterranean scrub and its foot indented with turquoise coves accessible only by boat or steep paths — the one stretch of unspoilt cliff coast on the otherwise flat Adriatic shore. Ancona is a port city of genuine grandeur, and the inland Sibillini foothills give way to medieval villages producing the excellent Verdicchio dei Castelli di Jesi white wine.
trips.cover_search_query: Monte Conero white cliffs Adriatic Marche Italy

trips.name: Food Valley: Parma, Modena & Bologna
trips.description: The Po Valley between Bologna, Parma, and Modena is the greatest concentration of protected food traditions in the world — Parmigiano-Reggiano, Prosciutto di Parma, balsamic vinegar of Modena, tortellini, mortadella, and tagliatelle al ragù all originate within a 100-kilometre radius. Bologna's arcaded streets and Europe's oldest university make it one of Italy's most rewarding cities for the genuinely curious visitor.
trips.cover_search_query: Bologna Piazza Maggiore medieval arcades Italy

trips.name: Motor Valley: Ferrari, Lamborghini & Ducati
trips.description: A triangle of countryside between Modena, Bologna, and Imola gave birth to the greatest concentration of performance car and motorcycle manufacturers on earth — Ferrari, Lamborghini, Maserati, Pagani, and Ducati all have factories, museums, and race tracks within 50 kilometres of each other. Maranello is the holy city for every Ferrari devotee, and the Autodromo di Imola hosted Formula 1 for decades.
trips.cover_search_query: Ferrari Museum Maranello Italy

trips.name: Ravenna & the Adriatic
trips.description: Ravenna was three times the capital of an empire — Western Roman, Ostrogothic, and Byzantine — and its eight UNESCO-listed monuments include the most astonishing Early Christian and Byzantine mosaics in the world, their gold and lapis-lazuli tesserae still brilliant after 1,500 years. The Po Delta nearby is one of Italy's great wetland nature reserves, where the river fragments into dozens of channels through reed beds haunted by herons and flamingos.
trips.cover_search_query: Ravenna Byzantine mosaics Basilica Italy

trips.name: Langhe Wine Country
trips.description: The Langhe hills south of Turin produce Barolo and Barbaresco, Italy's two most revered red wines — and the landscape of vine-striped ridges, hilltop villages, and medieval towers that frames the vineyards is among the most beautiful wine country on earth, recognised by UNESCO in 2014. Alba in October during the white truffle season is one of Italy's great sensory experiences: intense porcini and truffle aromas filling a medieval city humming with Michelin-starred tables.
trips.cover_search_query: Barolo vineyards Langhe Piedmont Italy

trips.name: Aosta Valley: Mont Blanc & Gran Paradiso
trips.description: The Aosta Valley is Italy's smallest and highest region — a mountain corridor enclosed by the highest peaks in the Alps. Mont Blanc, Monte Rosa, the Matterhorn, and Gran Paradiso all tower over this narrow valley where Roman ruins, medieval castles, and French patois coexist in a setting of dramatic alpine grandeur. Gran Paradiso National Park, Italy's oldest, is one of the best places in Europe to see ibex at close range.
trips.cover_search_query: Aosta Valley mountain castle Valle d'Aosta Italy

trips.name: Turin & the Royal Baroque
trips.description: Turin is one of Italy's most underrated cities — a Baroque capital of broad boulevards, grand arcaded streets, and palaces that housed the House of Savoy for centuries. The city's chocolate and coffee culture is legendary: bicerin (hot chocolate with espresso and cream) was invented here, and the historic cafes of Via Po have been philosophical salons since the Enlightenment. The Shroud of Turin and one of Italy's finest Egyptian museums complete a city full of surprises.
trips.cover_search_query: Turin Mole Antonelliana Piedmont Italy

trips.name: Monferrato, Asti & the Canonical Hills
trips.description: The Monferrato hills between Asti and Alessandria are the quiet twin of the more famous Langhe — producing Barbera and Grignolino wines, dotted with Romanesque churches in every village, and scattered with the hilltop castles of a landscape that changed hands between Montferrat, Milan, and Savoy for five centuries. Asti is famous for its Spumante sparkling wine and one of Italy's oldest horse races, the Palio, run on the main piazza since 1275.
trips.cover_search_query: Monferrato vineyards hilltop Piedmont Italy

trips.name: Rome & the Castelli Romani
trips.description: Rome needs no introduction as the most historically layered city on earth — two and a half millennia of continuous habitation have produced a city where ancient, medieval, Renaissance, and Baroque collide at every step. The Castelli Romani hill towns south of Rome have been the summer escape of popes and Roman families since antiquity: Frascati's white wine, Castel Gandolfo's papal villa above the lake, and Nemi's strawberry festival make for ideal excursions.
trips.cover_search_query: Rome Colosseum ancient Italy

trips.name: Etruscan Lazio & Viterbo
trips.description: Northern Lazio is the homeland of the Etruscans — a pre-Roman civilisation of enormous sophistication whose painted tombs, carved road systems, and hilltop necropolises are still being excavated. Viterbo is a beautiful medieval papal city with thermal baths flowing directly from the ground; nearby Bomarzo's Park of Monsters contains one of the most extraordinary Renaissance curiosities in Italy — a garden of giant stone monsters commissioned by a grieving nobleman in 1552.
trips.cover_search_query: Civita di Bagnoregio hilltop village Lazio Italy

trips.name: Gran Sasso & Abruzzo Highlands
trips.description: Gran Sasso d'Italia is the highest peak in the Apennines, and the national park surrounding it protects the last Apennine wolves, Marsican brown bears, and chamois in a landscape of alpine meadows, medieval shepherd villages, and beech forests. The stone village of Santo Stefano di Sessanio and the nearby Rocca Calascio fortress are among the most photogenic medieval sites in Italy, seemingly unchanged since the Middle Ages.
trips.cover_search_query: Rocca Calascio fortress Gran Sasso Abruzzo Italy

trips.name: Trabocchi Coast
trips.description: The Trabocchi Coast of Abruzzo is named for the extraordinary wooden fishing machines — trabocchi — that jut from the cliffs on spider-like stilts, their long poles and nets unchanged since the 17th century. Many are now converted into restaurants where you eat fish caught metres away. The Via Verde cycling path converted from a disused railway line runs the full length of the coast with the Adriatic on one side and the Maiella massif on the other.
trips.cover_search_query: Trabocco fishing machine Abruzzo coast Italy

trips.name: Matera & Basilicata
trips.description: Matera is one of Italy's most startling cities — a UNESCO World Heritage Site of cave dwellings (sassi) carved from a ravine, inhabited continuously since the Palaeolithic era and forcibly evacuated in the 1950s when the government declared it a national shame. Today the sassi are converted to extraordinary boutique hotels and restaurants. Nearby Metaponto has one of the finest Greek temple sites in southern Italy.
trips.cover_search_query: Matera sassi cave city Basilicata Italy

trips.name: Tropea & the Tyrrhenian Coast
trips.description: Tropea is one of southern Italy's most dramatically situated towns — a clifftop citadel above a beach whose water runs in improbable Caribbean shades of turquoise and emerald. The whole Tyrrhenian coast of Calabria is rugged and largely overlooked, with Greek ruins at Locri, the bronze warriors of Reggio Calabria, and the bergamot-scented citrus groves of the Costa Viola.
trips.cover_search_query: Tropea clifftop old town sea Calabria Italy

trips.name: Trieste & Mitteleuropa
trips.description: Trieste is Italy's most un-Italian city — a Habsburg port of grand neoclassical boulevards, Central European coffee house culture, and a literary heritage that includes James Joyce, Italo Svevo, and Rainer Maria Rilke, all of whom lived here. The Karst plateau behind the city drops to the sea in white limestone cliffs, and the Miramare castle perches on a headland above the Gulf of Trieste in a setting of romantic desolation.
trips.cover_search_query: Trieste Piazza Unità d'Italia sea Italy

trips.name: Bergamo, Brescia & Franciacorta
trips.description: Bergamo's upper town (Città Alta) is one of northern Italy's most beautifully preserved medieval cities, enclosed in 16th-century Venetian walls with funicular access and views over the Lombard plain. Brescia is the Roman capital of northern Italy with an extraordinary archaeological site in its centre. Between the two cities, the moraine hills of Franciacorta produce Italy's finest méthode champenoise sparkling wine.
trips.cover_search_query: Bergamo Città Alta medieval walls Lombardy Italy

trips.name: Valtellina & the Alpine Valley
trips.description: The Valtellina is the long valley of the Adda river cutting east into the Alps from Lake Como — a landscape of vertiginous terraced vineyards producing Nebbiolo wine (here called Sassella, Grumello, and Inferno), glacier-fed mountain streams, and the distinctive alpine architecture of Bormio and Tirano. The UNESCO-listed Bernina railway crosses the watershed to St Moritz from Tirano in one of the world's most spectacular train journeys.
trips.cover_search_query: Valtellina terraced vineyards Alps Lombardy Italy

trips.name: Brenta Dolomites & Adamello
trips.description: The Brenta Dolomites rise as an isolated massif west of the main Dolomite chain — more remote, wilder, and dramatically different in character from the eastern ranges. Trento is one of northern Italy's finest Renaissance cities, the seat of the Counter-Reformation Council that reshaped European Catholicism. Lake Garda's northern tip is overlooked by the Brenta from the west, and the Adamello glacier park preserves a remarkable First World War high-altitude frontier.
trips.cover_search_query: Brenta Dolomites Madonna di Campiglio Trentino Italy
